verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “When they therefore were come together, they asked of Him, saying, Lord, wilt Thou at this time restore again the kingdom to Israel?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When the disciples intend to ask anything, they approach Him together, that by dint of numbers they may abash Him into compliance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They well knew that in what He had said previously, “Of that day knoweth no man” (Matt. xxiv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 36), He had merely declined telling them: therefore they again drew near, and put the question.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They would not have put it had they been truly satisfied with that answer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For having heard that they were about to receive the Holy Ghost, they, as being now worthy of instruction, desired to learn.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Also they were quite ready for freedom: for they had no mind to address themselves to danger; what they wished was to breathe freely again; for they were no light matters that had happened to them, but the utmost peril had impended over them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And without saying any thing to Him of the Holy Ghost, they put this question: “Lord, wilt Thou at this time restore the kingdom to Israel?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They did not ask, when? but whether “at this time.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So eager were they for that day.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Indeed, to me it appears that they had not any clear notion of the nature of that kingdom; for the Spirit had not yet instructed them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they do not say, When shall these things be? but they approach Him with greater honour, saying, “Wilt Thou at this time restore again the kingdom,” as being now already fallen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there they were still affected towards sensible objects, seeing they were not yet become better than those who were before them; here they have henceforth high conceptions concerning Christ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since then their minds are elevated, He also speaks to them in a higher strain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For He no longer tells them, “Of that day not even the Son of Man knoweth” (Mark xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 32); but He says, It is not for you to know the times or the seasons which the Father hath put in His own power (Acts i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ye ask things greater than your capacity, He would say.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet even now they learned things that were much greater than this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that you may see that this is strictly the case, look how many things I shall enumerate.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What, I pray you, was greater than their having learned what they did learn?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus, they learned that there is a Son of God, and that God has a Son equal with Himself in dignity (John v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17–20); they learned that there will be a resurrection (Matt. xvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 9); that when He ascended He sat on the right hand of God (Luke xxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 69); and what is still more stupendous, that Flesh is seated in heaven, and adored by Angels, and that He will come again (Mark xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 19); they learned what is to take place in the judgment (Matt. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 27); learned that they shall then sit and judge the twelve tribes of Israel (Luke xxi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 27); learned that the Jews would be cast out, and in their stead the Gentiles should come in (Matt. xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, tell me, which is greater? to learn that a person will reign, or to learn the time when?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Paul learned “things which it is not lawful for a man to utter” (2 Cor. xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4); things that were before the world was made, he learned them all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Which is the more difficult, the beginning or the end?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Clearly to learn the beginning.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This, Moses learned, and the time when, and how long ago: and he enumerates the years.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the wise Solomon saith, “I will make mention of things from the beginning of the world.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that the time is at hand, they do know: as Paul saith, “The Lord is at hand, be careful for nothing.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things they knew not [then], and yet He mentions many signs (Matt. ch. xxiv).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, as He has just said, “Not many days hence,” wishing them to be vigilant, and did not openly declare the precise moment, so is it here also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: However, it is not about the general Consummation that they now ask Him, but, “Wilt Thou at this time,” say they, “restore the kingdom to Israel?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And not even this did He reveal to them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They also asked this [about the end of the world] before: and as on that occasion He answered by leading them away from thinking that their deliverance was near and, on the contrary, cast them into the midst of perils, so likewise on this occasion but more mildly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, that they may not imagine themselves to be wronged, and these things to be mere pretences, hear what He says: He immediately gives them that at which they rejoiced: for He adds: “But ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is come upon you; and ye shall be witnesses unto Me both in Jerusalem, and in all Judea, and in Samaria, and unto the uttermost part of the earth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, that they may make no more enquiries, straightway He was received up.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus, just as on the former occasion He had darkened their minds by awe, and by saying, “I know not;” here also He does so by being taken up.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For great was their eagerness on the subject, and they would not have desisted; and yet it was very necessary that they should not learn this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For tell me, which do the Gentiles most disbelieve? that there will be a consummation of the world, or that God is become man, and issued from the Virgin?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But I am ashamed of dwelling on this point, as if it were about some difficult matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then again, that the disciples might not say, Why dost thou leave the matter in suspense?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He adds, “Which the Father hath put in His own power.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet He declared the Father’s power and His to be one: as in the saying, “For as the Father raiseth up the dead and quickeneth them, even so the Son quickeneth whom He will.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If where need is to work, Thou actest with the same power as the Father; where it behooves to know, dost Thou not know with the same power?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yet certainly to raise up the dead is much greater than to learn the day.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If the greater be with power, much more the other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But just as when we see a child crying, and pertinaciously wishing to get something from us that is not expedient for him, we hide the thing, and show him our empty hands, and say, “See, we have it not:” the like has Christ here done with the Apostles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But as the child, even when we show him [our empty hands], persists with his crying, conscious he has been deceived, and then we leave him, and depart, saying, “Such an one calls me:” and we give him something else instead, in order to divert him from his desire, telling him it is a much finer thing than the other, and then hasten away; in like manner Christ acted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The disciples asked to have something, and He said He had it not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And on the first occasion he frightened them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then again they asked to have it now: He said He had it not; and He did not frighten them now, but after having shown [the empty hands], He has done this, and gives them a plausible reason: “Which the Father,” He says, “hath put in his own power.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou not know the things of the Father!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou knowest Him, and not what belongs to Him!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet Thou hast said, “None knoweth the Father but the Son” (Luke x.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 25); and, “The Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the deep things of God” (1 Cor. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10); and Thou not know this!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they feared to ask Him again, lest they should hear Him say, “Are ye also without understanding?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they feared Him now much more than before.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is come upon you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As in the former instance He had not answered their question (for it is the part of a teacher to teach not what the disciple chooses, but what is expedient for him to learn), so in this, He tells them beforehand, for this reason, what they ought to know, that they may not be troubled.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In truth, they were yet weak.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But to inspire them with confidence, He raised up their souls, and concealed what was grievous.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since he was about to leave them very shortly, therefore in this discourse He says nothing painful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He extols as great the things which would be painful: all but saying, “‘Fear not’: for ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is come upon you; and ye shall be witnesses unto Me both in Jerusalem, and in all Judea, and in Samaria.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For since he had said, “Go not into the way of the Gentiles, and into any city of the Samaritans enter ye not” (Matt. x.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 5), what there He left unsaid, He here adds, “And to the uttermost part of the earth;” and having spoken this, which was more fearful than all the rest, then that they may not again question Him, He held His peace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having this said, while they beheld, He was taken up; and a cloud received Him out of their sight” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou that they did preach and fulfil the Gospel?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For great was the gift He had bestowed on them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the very place, He says, where ye are afraid, that is, in Jerusalem, there preach ye first, and afterwards unto the uttermost part of the earth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then for assurance of what He had said, “While they beheld, He was taken up.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not “while they beheld” did He rise from the dead, but “while they beheld, He was taken up.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Inasmuch, however, as the sight of their eyes even here was not all-sufficient; for in the Resurrection they saw the end, but not the beginning, and in the Ascension they saw the beginning, but not the end: because in the former it had been superfluous to have seen the beginning, the Lord Himself Who spake these things being present, and the sepulchre showing clearly that He is not there; but in the latter, they needed to be informed of the sequel by word of others: inasmuch then as their eyes do not suffice to show them the height above, nor to inform them whether He is actually gone up into heaven, or only seemingly into heaven, see then what follows.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That it was Jesus Himself they knew from the fact that He had been conversing with them (for had they seen only from a distance, they could not have recognized Him by sight), but that He is taken up into Heaven the Angels themselves inform them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how it is ordered, that not all is done by the Spirit, but the eyes also do their part.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But why did “a cloud receive Him?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This too was a sure sign that He went up to Heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not fire, as in the case of Elijah, nor fiery chariot, but “a cloud received Him;” which was a symbol of Heaven, as the Prophet says; “Who maketh the clouds His chariot” (Ps. civ.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 3); it is of the Father Himself that this is said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore he says, “on a cloud;” in the symbol, he would say, of the Divine power, for no other Power is seen to appear on a cloud.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For hear again what another Prophet says: “The Lord sitteth upon a light cloud” (Is. xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it was while they were listening with great attention to what He was saying, and this in answer to a very interesting question, and with their minds fully aroused and quite awake, that this thing took place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Also on the mount [Sinai] the cloud was because of Him: since Moses also entered into the darkness, but the cloud there was not because of Moses.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And He did not merely say, “I go,” lest they should again grieve, but He said, “I send the Spirit” (John xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 5, 7); and that He was going away into heaven they saw with their eyes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: O what a sight they were granted!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And while they looked stedfastly,” it is said, “toward heaven, as he went up, behold, two men stood by them in white apparel; which also said, Ye men of Galilee, why stand ye gazing up into heaven?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This same Jesus, which is taken up from you into heaven”—they used the expression “This” demonstratively, saying, “this Jesus, which is taken up from you into heaven, shall thus”—demonstratively, “in this way”—“come in like manner as ye have seen Him going into heaven.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, the outward appearance is cheering [“in white apparel”].

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They were Angels, in the form of men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they say, “Ye men of Galilee:” they showed themselves to be trusted by the disciples, by saying, “Ye men of Galilee.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this was the meaning: else, what needed they to be told of their country, who knew it well enough?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By their appearance also they attracted their regard, and shewed that they were from heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But wherefore does not Christ Himself tell them these things, instead of the Angels?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He had beforehand told them all things; [“What if ye shall see the Son of Man] going up where He was before?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover the Angels did not say, ‘whom you have seen taken up,’ but, “going into heaven:” ascension is the word, not assumption; the expression “taken up,” belongs to the flesh.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the same reason they say, “He which is taken up from you shall thus come,” not, “shall be sent,” but, “shall come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that ascended, the same is he also that descended” (Eph. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So again the expression, “a cloud received Him:” for He Himself mounted upon the cloud.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of the expressions, some are adapted to the conceptions of the disciples, some agreeable with the Divine Majesty.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now, as they behold, their conceptions are elevated: He has given them no slight hint of the nature of His second coming.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this, “Shall thus come,” means, with the body; which thing they desired to hear; and, that he shall come again to judgment “thus” upon a cloud.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And, behold, two men stood by them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why is it said, “men?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because they had fashioned themselves completely as such, that the beholders might not be overpowered.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which also said:” their words moreover were calculated for soothing: “Why stand ye gazing up into heaven?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They would not let them any longer wait there for Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here again, these tell what is greater, and leave the less unsaid.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That “He will thus come,” they say, and that “ye must look for Him from heaven.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the rest, they called them off from that spectacle to their saying, that they might not, because they could not see Him, imagine that He was not ascended, but even while they are conversing, would be present ere they were aware.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if they said on a former occasion, “Whither goest Thou?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 36) much more would they have said it now.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Wilt Thou at this time,” say they, “restore the kingdom to Israel?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They so well knew his mildness, that after His Passion also they ask Him, “Wilt thou restore?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet He had before said to them, “Ye shall hear of wars and rumors of wars, but the end is not yet,” nor shall Jerusalem be taken.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But now they ask Him about the kingdom, not about the end.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And besides, He does not speak at great length with them after the Resurrection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They address then this question, as thinking that they themselves would be in high honor, if this should come to pass.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But He (for as touching this restoration, that it was not to be, He did not openly declare; for what needed they to learn this? hence they do not again ask, “What is the sign of Thy coming, and of the end of the world?” for they are afraid to say that: but, “Wilt Thou restore the kingdom to Israel?” for they thought there was such a kingdom), but He, I say, both in parables had shown that the time was not near, and here where they asked, and He answered thereto, “Ye shall receive power,” says He, “when the Holy Ghost is come upon you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is come upon you,” not, “is sent,” [to shew the Spirit’s coequal Majesty.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then darest thou, O opponent of the Spirit, to call Him a creature?].

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And ye shall be witnesses to Me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He hinted at the Ascension. [“And when he had spoken these things.]

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Which they had heard before, and He now reminds them of. [“He was taken up.”]

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Already it has been shown, that He went up into heaven. [“And a cloud, etc.”] “Clouds and darkness are under His feet,” (Ps. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 2) saith the Scripture: for this is declared by the expression, “And a cloud received Him:” the Lord of heaven, it means.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as a king is shown by the royal chariot, so was the royal chariot sent for Him. [“Behold, two men, etc.] That they may vent no sorrowful exclamations, and that it might not be with them as it was with Elisha, (2 Kings ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12) who, when his master was taken up, rent his mantle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what say they?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “This Jesus, which is taken up from you into heaven, shall thus come.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And, “Behold, two men stood by them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With good reason: for “in the mouth of two witnesses shall every word be established” (Deut. xvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 6): and these utter the same things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it is said, that they were “in white apparel.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the same manner as they had already seen an Angel at the sepulchre, who had even told them their own thoughts; so here also an Angel is the preacher of His Ascension; although indeed the Prophets had frequently foretold it, as well as the Resurrection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Everywhere it is Angels as at the Nativity, “for that which is conceived in her,” saith one, “is by the Holy Ghost” (Matt. i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20): and again to Mary, “Fear not, Mary.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And at the Resurrection: “He is not here; He is risen, and goeth before you.” (ib. xxiv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Come, and see!”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And at the Second Coming.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that they may not be utterly in amaze, therefore it is added, “Shall thus come.” (ib. xxv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They recover their breath a little; if indeed He shall come again, if also thus come, and not be unapproachable!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that expression also, that it is “from them” He is taken up, is not idly added.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And of the Resurrection indeed Christ Himself bears witness (because of all things this is, next to the Nativity, nay even above the Nativity, the most wonderful: His raising Himself to life again): for, “Destroy,” He says, “this Temple, and in three days I will raise it up.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Shall thus come,” say they.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If any therefore desires to see Christ; if any grieves that he has not seen Him: having this heard, let him show forth an admirable life, and certainly he shall see Him, and shall not be disappointed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For Christ will come with greater glory, though “thus,” in this manner, with a body; and much more wondrous will it be to see Him descending from heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But for what He will come, they do not add.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is a confirmation of the Resurrection; for if he was taken up with a body, much rather must He have risen again with a body.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Where are those who disbelieve the Resurrection?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who are they, I pray?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Are they Gentiles, or Christians? for I am ignorant.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But no, I know well: they are Gentiles, who also disbelieve the work of Creation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the two denials go together: the denial that God creates any thing from nothing, and the denial that He raises up what has been buried.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But then, being ashamed to be thought such as “know not the power of God” (Matt. xxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 29), that we may not impute this to them, they allege: We do not say it with this meaning, but because there is no need of the body.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Truly it may be seasonably said, “The fool will speak foolishness.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Are you not ashamed not to grant, that God can create from nothing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If he creates from matter already existing, wherein does He differ from men?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But whence, you demand, are evils?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Though you should not know whence, ought you for that to introduce another evil in the knowledge of evils?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hereupon two absurdities follow.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if you do not grant, that from things which are not, God made the things which are, much more shall you be ignorant whence are evils: and then, again, you introduce another evil, the affirming that Evil (τὴν κακίαν) is uncreated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Consider now what a thing it is, when you wish to find the source of evils, to be both ignorant of it, and to add another to it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Search after the origin of evils, and do not blaspheme God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how do I blaspheme? says he.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When you make out that evils have a power equal to God’s; a power uncreated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, observe what Paul says; “For the invisible things of Him from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the devil would have both to be of matter, that there may be nothing left from which we may come to the knowledge of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For tell me, whether is harder: to take that which is by nature evil (if indeed there be ought such; for I speak upon your principles, since there is no such thing as evil by nature), and make it either good, or even coefficent of good? or, to make of nothing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whether is easier (I speak of quality); to induce the non-existent quality; or to take the existing quality, and change it into its contrary? where there is no house, to make the house; or where it is utterly destroyed, to make it identically exist again?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, as this is impossible, so is that: to make a thing into its opposite.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tell me, whether is harder; to make a perfume, or to make filth have the effect of perfume?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say, whether of these is easier (since we subject God to our reasonings: nay, not we, but ye); to form eyes, or to make a blind man to see continuing blind, and yet more sharp-sighted, than one who does see?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To make blindness into sight, and deafness into hearing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To me the other seems easier.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say then do you grant God that which is harder, and not grant the easier?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But souls also they affirm to be of His substance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see what a number of impieties and absurdities are here!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the first place, wishing to show that evils are from God, they bring in another thing more impious than this, that they are equal with Him in majesty, and God prior in existence to none of them, assigning this great prerogative even to them!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the next place, they affirm evil to be indestructible: for if that which is uncreated can be destroyed, ye see the blasphemy!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that it comes to this, either that nothing is of God if not these; or that these are God!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thirdly, what I have before spoken of, in this point they defeat themselves, and prepare against themselves fresh indignation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Fourthly, they affirm unordered matter to possess such inherent (ἐπιτηδειότητα) power.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Fifthly, that evil is the cause of the goodness of God, and that without this the Good had not been good.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Sixthly, they bar against us the ways of attaining unto the knowledge of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seventhly, they bring God down into men, yea plants and logs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if our soul be of the substance of God, but the process of its transmigration into new bodies brings it at last into cucumbers, and melons, and onions, why then the substance of God will pass into cucumbers!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And if we say, that the Holy Ghost fashioned the Temple [of our Lord’s body] in the Virgin, they laugh us to scorn: and if, that He dwelt in that spiritual Temple, again they laugh; while they themselves are not ashamed to bring down God’s substance into cucumbers, and melons, and flies, and caterpillars, and asses, thus excogitating a new fashion of idolatry: for let it not be as the Egyptians have it, “The onion is God;” but let it be, “God in the onion”!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why dost thou shrink from the notion of God’s entering into a body?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: ‘It is shocking,’ says he.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then this is much more shocking.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, forsooth, it is not shocking—how should it be?—this same thing which is so, if it be into us!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: ‘But thy notion is indeed shocking.’

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do ye see the filthiness of their impiety?—But why do they not wish the body to be raised?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And why do they say the body is evil?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By what then, tell me, dost thou know God? by what hast thou the knowledge of existing things?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The philosopher too: by means of what is he a philosopher, if the body does nothing towards it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Deaden the senses, and then learn something of the things one needs to know!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What would be more foolish than a soul, if from the first it had the senses deadened?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If the deadening of but a single part, I mean of the brain, becomes a marring of it altogether; if all the rest should be deadened, what would it be good for?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Show me a soul without a body.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you not hear physicians say, The presence of disease sadly enfeebles the soul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How long will ye put off hanging yourselves?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is the body material? tell me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “To be sure, it is.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then you ought to hate it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why do you feed, why cherish it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You ought to get quit of this prison.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But besides: “God cannot overcome matter, unless he (συμπλακἥ) implicate himself with it: for he cannot issue orders to it (O feebleness!) until he close with it, and (σταθἥ) take his stand (say you) through the whole of it!”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And a king indeed does all by commanding; but God, not by commanding the evil!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In short, if it were unparticipant of all good, it could not subsist at all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For Evil cannot subsist, unless it lay hold upon somewhat of the accidents of Virtue: so that if it had been heretofore all unmixed with virtue, it would have perished long ago: for such is the condition of evils.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let there be a profligate man, let him put upon himself no restraint whatever, will he live ten days?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let there be a robber, and devoid of all conscience in his dealings with every one, let him be such even to his fellow-robbers, will he be able to live?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let there be a thief, void of all shame, who knows not what blushing is, but steals openly in public.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not in the nature of evils to subsist, unless they get some small share at least in good.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that hereupon, according to these men, God gave them their subsistence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let there be a city of wicked men; will it stand?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let them be wicked, not only with regard to the good, but towards each other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, it is impossible such a city should stand.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Truly, “professing themselves to be wise, they became fools.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If bodily substance be evil, then all things visible exist idly, and in vain, both water and earth, and sun, and air; for air is also body, though not solid.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is in point then to say, “The wicked have told me foolish things.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let not us endure them, let us block up our ears against them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there is, yea, there is, a resurrection of bodies.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This the sepulchre which is at Jerusalem declares, this the pillar to which He was bound, when He was scourged.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, “We did eat and drink with Him,” it is said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us then believe in the Resurrection, and do things worthy of it, that we may attain to the good things which are to come, through Christ Jesus our Lord, with Whom to the Father, and the Holy Ghost together, be power, honor, now and for ever, world without end.
